The land enjoyed peace for eighty years, which should have strengthened their faith; but it made them careless and indulge in selfish desires. This is how the prosperity of fools destroys them. Jabin and his general Sisera greatly oppressed Israel. This enemy was closer than any of the previous ones. Israel cried out to the Lord when distress drove them to him, and they saw no other way to be helped. Those who ignore God in times of prosperity will find themselves needing to seek him in trouble.

Deborah was a prophetess; someone instructed in divine knowledge by the inspiration of the Spirit of God. She judged Israel as God’s voice to them; correcting injustices and resolving complaints. By God's direction, she ordered Barak to raise an army and fight Jabin's forces. Barak insisted that she be present. Deborah promised to go with him. She would not send him where she herself would not go. Those who call others to their duty in God’s name should be ready to assist them in it. Barak valued the satisfaction of his mind and the success of his plan more than mere honor.

Sisera's confidence was mainly in his chariots. But if we have reason to hope that God goes before us, we can go forward with courage and cheerfulness. Do not be discouraged by the difficulties you encounter when resisting Satan, serving God, or suffering for him; for is not the Lord gone before you? Follow him then fully. Barak went down, even though on the open plain the chariots would have an advantage against him; he left the mountain in dependence on divine power; for in the Lord alone is the salvation of his people. He was not disappointed in his confidence. When God goes before us in our spiritual conflicts, we must be active; and when, by his grace, he gives us some success against the enemies of our souls, we must improve it with watchfulness and determination.

Sisera's chariots had been his pride and his confidence. This is how those who rely on created things are disappointed; like a broken reed, it not only breaks under them but pierces them with many sorrows. An idol can quickly become a burden; what we desire, God can make us dislike. It is likely that Jael initially intended kindness toward Sisera; but by divine impulse she was later led to consider him as the determined enemy of the Lord and his people, and to destroy him. All our connections with God’s enemies must be broken off if we want the Lord for our God and his people for our people. He who thought to destroy Israel with his many iron chariots is himself destroyed with one iron nail. Thus the weak things of the world confound the mighty. The Israelites would have prevented much harm if they had sooner destroyed the Canaanites, as God commanded and enabled them; but it is better to be wise late and buy wisdom by experience than never to be wise.
